title: Display & Edit Methods in X++
summary: Master display and edit methods to create calculated fields, custom UI behaviors, and data transformations in D365 F&O forms and reports.

content.overview:
Display and edit methods are one of X++'s most powerful features for creating calculated fields and custom UI behaviors without modifying base application tables. These methods execute on-demand when forms render or reports generate, allowing you to present transformed data, perform calculations, or control field editability based on runtime conditions. Unlike stored database fields, display/edit methods are computed in real-time, making them perfect for derived values, status indicators, and conditional formatting.

In D365 F&O architecture, display methods are read-only calculated fields that return values for display purposes, while edit methods provide two-way data binding for complex field interactions. They're essential for customizations that need to present data differently than it's stored, implement business logic for field validation, or create composite fields that combine multiple table fields. Understanding their execution context, caching behavior, and performance implications is crucial for building responsive, maintainable solutions.
content.theory:
Display and edit methods are declared with the [Display] and [Edit] attributes respectively, and must follow specific naming and signature conventions. The framework automatically discovers these methods through reflection and binds them to form controls or report fields based on the Extended Data Type (EDT) specified in the attribute. The method execution occurs within the table's context, giving you access to all table fields and methods through the 'this' reference.

The caching mechanism is critical to understand: display methods are cached per record until the record's data changes or the cache is explicitly invalidated using the table's reread() method. This prevents redundant calculations but can cause stale data if external dependencies change. The SysTableBrowser class and form data sources handle this caching automatically, but custom scenarios may require manual cache management.

For form integration, display/edit methods bound to form controls automatically participate in the form's data binding lifecycle. The FormDataSource calls these methods during record navigation, refresh operations, and field validation. Edit methods receive the new value as a parameter and must handle validation, transformation, and persistence to underlying table fields. They should call the table's validateField() method to trigger standard validation frameworks.

code_examples.title: Basic Display Method with Business Logic
code_examples.description: A display method on CustTable that calculates customer credit utilization percentage with proper error handling and null checking

code_examples.title: Advanced Edit Method with Validation
code_examples.description: An edit method that handles complex field interactions, validation, and automatic field updates when dependencies change

code_examples.title: Performance-Optimized Display Method with Caching
code_examples.description: A complex display method that implements custom caching and batch data retrieval to avoid N+1 query problems

key_points: Display methods are read-only and execute on-demand — they should never modify table data directly, only return calculated or transformed values for presentation
Edit methods provide bidirectional data binding — the boolean parameter indicates if it's a get (_set=false) or set (_set=true) operation, and you must handle both scenarios
Method signatures must match exact patterns — display methods return the EDT type and take no parameters, edit methods return EDT type and take boolean + value parameters
Caching behavior is automatic but controllable — the framework caches results per record until data changes, but you can force refresh with table.reread() or implement custom caching
Performance implications are significant — display methods execute for every visible record in grids, so optimize queries and consider batch loading for expensive operations
gotchas: Display methods in grids execute for EVERY visible row — if your grid shows 50 records and your display method does a database query, that's 50 queries. Use static caching or preload data to avoid N+1 problems
Edit method validation exceptions don't automatically rollback — wrap database modifications in ttsbegin/ttscommit blocks and handle rollback explicitly in catch blocks to maintain data consistency

Recursive display method calls cause stack overflow — if display method A calls a method that triggers display method B, which calls back to A, you'll crash. Use static flags or redesign the dependency chain
performance_tips: Implement static caching for expensive display methods that show the same data across multiple records — use Maps with cache expiry logic to balance performance and data freshness
Use batch preloading in form initialization — call a static method that loads all required data in a single query, then cache results for individual display method calls

quiz.q: What happens when a display method is bound to a grid column showing 100 records?
options: The method executes once and the result is shared
The method executes 100 times, once per record
The method executes only for visible rows on screen
The method is cached and executes only when data changes
quiz.explanation: Display methods execute once for each record in the grid, which can cause performance issues. This is why caching and batch preloading are crucial for expensive operations.

quiz.q: In an edit method, how do you determine if you should return the current value or set a new value?
options: Check if the second parameter is null
Check the boolean _set parameter
Check if the method was called from a form
Check the table's current state
quiz.explanation: Edit methods use the boolean _set parameter to determine the operation mode: false means get/return current value, true means set/validate the new value provided in the second parameter.

quiz.q: What is the best way to handle database queries in display methods for optimal performance?
options: Use direct SQL queries instead of X++ select statements
Implement static caching with expiry logic
Always use firstonly in select statements
Call the display method recursively to build results
quiz.explanation: Static caching with expiry logic is the best approach because it prevents repeated database queries for the same data while ensuring data freshness through cache invalidation strategies.

title: Delegates & Event-Driven X++
summary: Master X++ delegates and event-driven programming to build loosely-coupled, extensible business logic in D365 F&O.

content.overview:
Delegates in X++ are type-safe function pointers that enable event-driven programming and the Observer pattern in D365 F&O. Unlike traditional inheritance-based extensibility, delegates provide a publish-subscribe mechanism where multiple subscribers can react to business events without tight coupling to the publisher. This is crucial for ISV solutions, customizations, and integrations that need to respond to standard business processes without modifying core Microsoft code.

Delegates are extensively used throughout D365 F&O's standard application, from form events (FormDataSourceEventArgs) to business logic events in classes like SalesTable and PurchTable. Understanding delegates is essential for modern X++ development, as they're the foundation for Chain of Command (CoC) extensibility and custom business event frameworks.

While delegates add a layer of indirection that can impact performance if misused, they're invaluable for building maintainable, loosely-coupled solutions that survive application updates and enable multiple customizations to coexist peacefully.
content.theory:
X++ delegates are implemented as special AOT objects under the \Classes node with the 'delegate' keyword. They define a contract (method signature) that subscriber methods must implement. The runtime maintains a subscription list for each delegate instance, invoking all subscribers sequentially when the delegate is called. This follows the multicast delegate pattern from .NET but with X++ semantics.

The D365 F&O framework uses delegates extensively in the Application Platform layer. For example, FormDataSource uses delegates like 'validatingWrite' and 'written' to allow customizations to participate in form data operations. The SysExtensionFramework leverages delegates for plugin architectures, and the Business Events framework uses delegates to publish integration events.

Delegates in X++ support both static and instance methods as subscribers. Static delegates are defined at the class level and share subscription lists across all instances, while instance delegates maintain per-object subscription lists. The subscription mechanism uses weak references to prevent memory leaks when subscriber objects are garbage collected. Understanding this distinction is crucial for avoiding subscription lifecycle issues in complex business scenarios.

code_examples.title: Basic Delegate Definition and Usage
code_examples.description: Creating a delegate for sales order validation with multiple subscribers

code_examples.title: Advanced Delegate Pattern with Error Handling
code_examples.description: Production-grade delegate usage with subscription management and error isolation

code_examples.title: Form Delegate Integration
code_examples.description: Integrating delegates with form events for dynamic UI behavior

key_points: Delegates enable loose coupling by allowing multiple subscribers to react to events without knowing about each other or the publisher
Use += and -= operators to subscribe/unsubscribe from delegates — always clean up subscriptions to prevent memory leaks
Static delegates share subscription lists across all instances, while instance delegates maintain per-object subscriptions
Delegate calls are synchronous and sequential — if one subscriber throws an exception, subsequent subscribers won't execute
FormDataSourceEventArgs and FormEventArgs are built on delegates, making them the foundation of modern D365 F&O form extensibility
gotchas: Memory leaks from orphaned subscriptions — always unsubscribe in finalize() or when the subscriber object is no longer needed, especially for static delegates
Exception propagation stops delegate chain — wrap delegate calls in try/catch to prevent one failing subscriber from breaking others
Recursive delegate calls can cause stack overflow — implement isProcessing flags to detect and prevent recursive event scenarios
performance_tips: Minimize delegate subscriber count and complexity since all subscribers execute synchronously on each delegate call
Use conditional logic in subscribers to exit early when processing isn't needed for the current context or record state

quiz.q: What happens when one subscriber method throws an unhandled exception during delegate execution?
options: All subscribers continue to execute normally
Subsequent subscribers in the chain are not executed
Only the first subscriber is affected
The delegate automatically retries the failed subscriber
quiz.explanation: When a subscriber throws an unhandled exception, the delegate call chain stops and subsequent subscribers are not executed. This is why production code should wrap delegate calls in try/catch blocks to isolate subscriber failures.

quiz.q: How do static delegates differ from instance delegates in terms of subscription management?
options: Static delegates maintain per-object subscription lists
Static delegates share subscription lists across all class instances
Instance delegates are faster than static delegates
There is no difference between static and instance delegates
quiz.explanation: Static delegates maintain a single, shared subscription list across all instances of the class, while instance delegates maintain separate subscription lists for each object instance. This affects memory management and subscription lifecycle.

quiz.q: Which operator is used to unsubscribe from a delegate in X++?
options: remove()
unsubscribe()
quiz.explanation: The -= operator is used to unsubscribe from delegates in X++, just like += is used to subscribe. This follows the same pattern as C# multicast delegate syntax.

title: Cross-Company Queries in X++
summary: Master cross-company data operations in D365 F&O using crossCompany, changeCompany, and advanced patterns for multi-entity scenarios.

content.overview: Cross-company queries are fundamental for multi-entity D365 F&O implementations where data must be accessed across legal entities. Unlike traditional single-company operations, cross-company scenarios require careful handling of company context, security boundaries, and performance considerations. D365 F&O provides several mechanisms including the crossCompany keyword, changeCompany statement, and Query framework extensions that enable developers to traverse company boundaries safely and efficiently. Understanding these patterns is crucial for enterprise implementations where consolidated reporting, inter-company transactions, and shared master data scenarios are common. The framework automatically handles company filtering in most cases, but cross-company operations require explicit developer intervention and proper security model implementation.
content.theory: D365 F&O's multi-company architecture is built on the concept of DataAreaId, which acts as a partition key for most transactional tables. The kernel maintains company context through the curext() function and CompanyInfo singleton, ensuring data isolation by default. Cross-company operations bypass this isolation through three primary mechanisms: the crossCompany keyword in select statements, the changeCompany statement for context switching, and the Query class's allowCrossCompany() method. The SysQuery framework provides additional cross-company capabilities through CrossCompanyQueryUtils and the QueryBuildDataSource.allowCrossCompany() method. Security is enforced through the XDSSecurityPolicy framework, which can restrict cross-company access based on user permissions and table-level security policies. Performance considerations include the fact that crossCompany queries cannot use indexes that include DataAreaId, forcing table scans in some scenarios. The RecordLevelSecurity framework also interacts with cross-company operations, requiring careful consideration of which companies a user can access through role-based security.

code_examples.title: Basic crossCompany Query with Error Handling
code_examples.description: Demonstrates fundamental cross-company query syntax with proper error handling and security validation

code_examples.title: Advanced Query Framework Cross-Company Pattern
code_examples.description: Production-grade cross-company implementation using Query framework with joins and complex filtering

code_examples.title: changeCompany Pattern for Cross-Company Updates
code_examples.description: Demonstrates safe cross-company data updates using changeCompany with transaction management

key_points: crossCompany keyword enables querying across multiple legal entities in a single select statement — use container of company IDs to limit scope
changeCompany statement switches execution context to another legal entity — essential for cross-company updates and maintaining data integrity
Query framework's allowCrossCompany() method provides programmatic control over cross-company operations with better performance for complex scenarios
Security validation must be performed explicitly for each target company using SecurityRights::hasTableAccess() before accessing cross-company data
Performance degrades significantly with crossCompany queries as DataAreaId-based indexes cannot be used — consider caching and limiting result sets
gotchas: crossCompany queries ignore company-specific customizations and extensions — CoC and event handlers may not fire as expected in target companies
Transaction boundaries with changeCompany can cause deadlocks — always use consistent company ordering and minimize transaction scope across companies
RecordLevelSecurity policies are evaluated in current company context, not target company — manually validate RLS rules when crossing company boundaries
performance_tips: Use specific company containers instead of querying all companies — CrossCompanyQueryUtils.getAllowedCompanies() respects user security but may return too many companies
Consider using SysOperation framework for long-running cross-company operations to avoid timeout issues and provide progress feedback to users

quiz.q: Which method is most efficient for updating the same record across multiple companies?
options: Multiple crossCompany select statements in a loop
Single changeCompany statement with company loop
Query framework with allowCrossCompany(true)
Separate batch jobs per company
quiz.explanation: changeCompany with a company loop is most efficient because it maintains proper transaction context, allows index usage within each company, and provides better error handling granularity compared to crossCompany select which cannot be used for updates.

quiz.q: What happens to RecordLevelSecurity when using crossCompany queries?
options: RLS policies are applied in each target company automatically
RLS is evaluated in the current company context only
RLS is completely bypassed for crossCompany operations
RLS policies must be manually specified in the query
quiz.explanation: RecordLevelSecurity policies are evaluated in the current company context only when using crossCompany queries. This means developers must manually validate security rules for each target company to ensure proper data access control.

quiz.q: Why do crossCompany queries typically have poor performance compared to single-company queries?
options: Network latency between companies
Additional security validation overhead
Cannot use indexes that include DataAreaId
Must load all company metadata first
quiz.explanation: crossCompany queries cannot use indexes that include DataAreaId as a key field, which forces table scans instead of efficient index seeks. Since most D365 tables include DataAreaId in their primary indexes, this significantly impacts query performance.

title: Containers: Pack, Unpack & Serialize
summary: Master X++ containers for efficient data serialization, multi-type collections, and parameter passing in D365 F&O development.

content.overview: Containers in X++ are heterogeneous data structures that can hold multiple values of different types in a single variable. Unlike arrays or lists, containers are immutable and use zero-based indexing, making them ideal for data serialization, parameter passing between tiers, and temporary data storage. They're extensively used in D365 F&O for batch job parameters, report data contracts, and inter-class communication where type safety isn't the primary concern. Understanding containers is crucial for working with legacy code, SysOperation framework, and scenarios requiring lightweight data transport across application boundaries.
content.theory: Containers are implemented as variant arrays in the kernel, stored as packed binary data that can efficiently serialize/deserialize across client-server boundaries. The SysContainer class provides helper methods, while the kernel functions conPeek(), conPoke(), conLen(), and conIns() offer direct manipulation. Containers excel in scenarios like SysOperationDataContractBase implementations, where RunBase-derived classes need to serialize parameters for batch execution. The pack() and unpack() pattern is fundamental to D365's batch framework, allowing complex objects to persist their state. However, containers sacrifice type safety for flexibility—there's no compile-time validation of element types, making them prone to runtime errors if not handled carefully. Modern D365 development favors strongly-typed data contracts over containers, but understanding them remains essential for framework integration and legacy system maintenance.

code_examples.title: Basic Container Operations and Type Safety
code_examples.description: Demonstrates fundamental container operations, type checking, and common manipulation patterns used in D365 batch jobs

code_examples.title: RunBase Pack/Unpack Pattern Implementation
code_examples.description: Production-grade example showing how containers serialize batch job parameters in the SysOperation framework

code_examples.title: Advanced Container Serialization for Complex Objects
code_examples.description: Shows how to serialize complex business objects using containers, including nested structures and collection handling

key_points: Containers are immutable — each modification creates a new container, making them memory-intensive for frequent updates
Use pack()/unpack() pattern with version numbers for backward-compatible serialization in RunBase classes
Always validate container length and element types before extraction to prevent runtime exceptions
conPeek() uses 1-based indexing, not 0-based like most programming languages
Containers excel for parameter serialization and cross-tier communication but lack compile-time type safety
gotchas: Container elements are not strongly typed — conPeek(con, 1) might return a string today and an integer tomorrow, causing runtime failures. Always use typeOf() validation before casting

Modifying containers in loops creates performance bottlenecks — each conPoke() or conIns() allocates a new container. Use List or Map collections for dynamic data, then convert to container when serialization is needed
Empty containers evaluate to false in boolean contexts, but conLen(conNull()) returns 0, not false — test with conLen(con) > 0 for clarity and consistency across different contexts
performance_tips: For large datasets, build containers once and pass them around rather than rebuilding — container creation has significant overhead compared to passing references
When deserializing complex objects, validate the entire container structure before extracting elements to fail fast and avoid partial state corruption

quiz.q: What happens when you execute conPoke(con, 2, 'newValue') on an existing container?
options: The original container is modified in place
A new container is created with the updated value
An exception is thrown
The operation is ignored
quiz.explanation: Containers are immutable in X++. conPoke() creates and returns a new container with the specified position updated, leaving the original container unchanged. This immutability is crucial for thread safety and serialization consistency.

quiz.q: In a RunBase.pack() method, why should you include a version number as the first element?
options: For debugging purposes only
To enable backward compatibility during upgrades
To improve serialization performance
It's required by the framework
quiz.explanation: Including a version number allows the unpack() method to handle different container structures gracefully. When the class evolves, you can detect older versions and provide appropriate defaults or migration logic, ensuring batch jobs don't break after code updates.

quiz.q: What is the most significant risk when using containers for data transport?
options: Poor performance compared to XML
Memory leaks from improper disposal
Loss of compile-time type safety
Incompatibility with batch processing
quiz.explanation: Containers sacrifice compile-time type safety for flexibility. You can store any type in any position, and conPeek() returns anytype. Without proper type validation using typeOf(), you risk runtime exceptions when assumptions about element types prove incorrect.

title: Reflection: DictTable, DictField APIs
summary: Master D365 F&O's reflection capabilities using DictTable and DictField APIs for runtime metadata introspection, dynamic form generation, and advanced integration scenarios.

content.overview: D365 F&O's reflection system provides powerful runtime introspection capabilities through the DictTable and DictField APIs, enabling developers to programmatically access table and field metadata without compile-time dependencies. This is essential for building generic frameworks, data migration tools, integration adapters, and dynamic UI components that must adapt to varying AOT structures across environments. Unlike .NET reflection, D365's reflection operates on AOT metadata cached in the application dictionary, making it extremely fast for metadata queries but limited to design-time structure information. Understanding these APIs is crucial for enterprise-level customizations that require flexibility and maintainability across multiple D365 installations with varying customizations.
content.theory: The reflection system in D365 F&O is built around the Dictionary classes hierarchy, with DictTable and DictField being the primary entry points for table and field metadata respectively. These classes provide access to the compiled AOT metadata stored in the UtilElements and related system tables, offering information about field types, labels, extended data types, relations, and validation rules. The DictTable class extends TreeNode and provides methods like fieldCnt(), fieldObject(), and relation() to traverse table structure, while DictField encapsulates field-specific metadata including baseType(), extendedDataType(), and label(). The reflection system integrates deeply with the SysTableBrowser framework, FormDataSource mechanics, and the broader metadata-driven architecture that powers features like personalization, data entities, and OData endpoints. Performance is optimized through aggressive caching of dictionary objects, but developers must understand the lifecycle implications and memory overhead of retaining references to these objects.

code_examples.title: Basic Table and Field Metadata Inspection
code_examples.description: Demonstrates how to iterate through table fields and extract comprehensive metadata including EDT information, labels, and field properties

code_examples.title: Dynamic Record Creation with Validation
code_examples.description: Advanced example showing how to use reflection for dynamic record creation with proper field validation and error handling

code_examples.title: Advanced Metadata Analysis for Integration Mapping
code_examples.description: Enterprise-grade example demonstrating how to build field mapping metadata for external system integration, including relation traversal and EDT hierarchy analysis

key_points: DictTable.makeRecord() creates properly initialized records with default values and field inheritance from EDT configurations — essential for programmatic record creation
Field iteration using fieldNext() is more efficient than fieldCnt() loops because it skips deleted field IDs and respects field inheritance hierarchy
DictField.isSystem() and DictField.mandatory() provide critical validation metadata for dynamic form generation and data import scenarios
Extended Data Type traversal through DictType.extends() enables building comprehensive field validation rules and UI hint systems
Table relations accessed via DictRelation provide foreign key metadata essential for building referential integrity in integration scenarios
gotchas: Caching DictTable and DictField objects can cause memory leaks in long-running processes — always scope these objects appropriately and avoid static references
fieldName2Id() is case-sensitive and will return 0 for non-existent fields, but won't throw exceptions — always validate return values before using field IDs
DictField.stringLen() returns the AOT-defined length, not the database column length, which can differ when using UTF-8 encoding — validate against actual database constraints in production
performance_tips: Pre-cache frequently accessed DictTable objects in class member variables rather than recreating them in loops — dictionary object creation involves AOT metadata lookup
Use fieldObject() sparingly in tight loops — consider caching field metadata in containers or maps for batch processing scenarios

quiz.q: What is the most efficient way to iterate through all non-system fields of a table using reflection APIs?
options: Use fieldCnt() and iterate from 1 to count
Use fieldNext() starting from 0 and check isSystem()
Use fieldObject() with sequential field IDs
Query the UtilElements table directly
quiz.explanation: fieldNext() is the proper iterator method that automatically skips deleted field IDs and respects the field hierarchy. Checking isSystem() allows filtering out system fields efficiently during iteration.

quiz.q: When using DictField.extendedDataType() to get EDT information, what should you check before using the returned value?
options: Check if the field is mandatory
Check if the return value is not 0
Check if the field is a system field
Check the field's base type
quiz.explanation: extendedDataType() returns 0 for fields that don't have an associated EDT (like primitive types). Always validate the return value before passing it to extendedTypeId2name() or creating DictType objects to avoid runtime errors.

quiz.q: What is the primary limitation of D365 F&O's reflection system compared to .NET reflection?
options: It cannot access private methods
It only provides compile-time metadata, not runtime state
It cannot create object instances
It doesn't support generic types
quiz.explanation: D365 F&O reflection operates on AOT metadata cached at application startup, providing design-time structure information but not runtime state or dynamic type creation capabilities like .NET reflection.

title: RunBase & RunBaseBatch Framework
summary: Master the RunBase framework for building standardized batch jobs and dialogs with parameter persistence, progress tracking, and query integration in D365 F&O.

content.overview:
The RunBase framework is the foundation for creating standardized batch jobs and interactive processes in D365 F&O. It provides a consistent pattern for parameter dialogs, query integration, progress tracking, and batch execution. RunBaseBatch extends RunBase to add full batch processing capabilities including server execution, retry logic, and error handling.

This framework is essential for building enterprise-grade processes that can run both interactively and in batch mode. It handles parameter persistence, user interface generation, and provides hooks for progress updates and error handling. Every major D365 process (like MRP, posting routines, and data imports) leverages this pattern.

Understanding RunBase is crucial for D365 developers as it's the standard way to build scalable, maintainable business processes that integrate seamlessly with the batch job framework and provide consistent user experiences across the application.
content.theory:
The RunBase framework follows the Template Method pattern, where the base class defines the execution flow and derived classes implement specific business logic. The framework consists of several key classes: RunBase (base functionality), RunBaseBatch (batch capabilities), and SysLastValue (parameter persistence). The execution flow follows a predictable pattern: dialog() builds the UI, getFromDialog() retrieves parameters, validate() checks inputs, and run() executes the business logic.

RunBase integrates deeply with the Query framework through the queryRun() method, allowing complex data selection criteria. The SysLastValue framework automatically persists dialog parameters using the class name and user ID as keys. For batch execution, RunBaseBatch adds server-side execution capabilities, retry logic through the BatchRetryable interface, and integration with the SysBatch framework.

The framework supports both synchronous (interactive) and asynchronous (batch) execution modes. In interactive mode, the process runs immediately with real-time progress updates. In batch mode, the job is queued for server execution with full error handling, logging, and retry capabilities. The canGoBatch() and canGoBatchJournal() methods control batch availability, while pack()/unpack() methods handle parameter serialization for server execution.

code_examples.title: Basic RunBase Implementation
code_examples.description: Complete RunBase class with dialog, parameter handling, and business logic execution

code_examples.title: RunBaseBatch with Progress Tracking
code_examples.description: Advanced RunBaseBatch implementation with progress tracking, error handling, and batch retry logic

key_points: RunBase provides a standardized framework for building dialogs and batch jobs with automatic parameter persistence via SysLastValue
Override key methods in sequence: dialog() for UI, getFromDialog() for parameter retrieval, validate() for input checking, and run() for execution
Use pack()/unpack() methods with versioning macros to ensure parameter serialization works correctly for batch execution
QueryRun integration allows complex data selection criteria that persist between dialog sessions and batch execution
RunBaseBatch extends RunBase with server-side execution, progress tracking, and retry capabilities through the BatchRetryable interface
gotchas: Forgetting to call super() in dialog(), getFromDialog(), and validate() methods breaks parameter persistence and validation chain
Not implementing pack()/unpack() correctly causes batch jobs to fail with serialization errors when parameters can't be transferred to the server
Using class variables without proper initialization in the constructor leads to inconsistent behavior between interactive and batch execution modes
performance_tips: Use set-based operations in run() method and commit transactions in batches (every 100-1000 records) to avoid lock escalation
Implement proper progress tracking with ProgressBase for long-running batch jobs to provide user feedback and enable cancellation

quiz.q: Which method in RunBase is responsible for building the parameter dialog UI?
options: getFromDialog()
dialog()
validate()
run()
quiz.explanation: The dialog() method is responsible for building the parameter dialog UI. It should call super() to get the base DialogRunbase object, then add custom fields and query builders as needed.

quiz.q: What happens if you don't implement pack() and unpack() methods in a RunBaseBatch class?
options: Dialog parameters are lost
Batch execution fails with serialization errors
Interactive mode doesn't work
Query selections are not persisted
quiz.explanation: Without proper pack()/unpack() implementation, batch execution fails because parameters cannot be serialized and transferred to the batch server. The framework needs these methods to reconstruct the object state on the server.

quiz.q: Which interface should a RunBaseBatch class implement to support automatic retry on failure?
options: Batchable
BatchRetryable
RunBaseRetry
BatchExecutable
quiz.explanation: The BatchRetryable interface should be implemented to support automatic retry on failure. It requires implementing the isRetryable() method that returns true if the job can be safely retried.

title: SysOperation Framework Deep Dive
summary: Master the SysOperation framework for building scalable, enterprise-grade batch operations and service endpoints in D365 F&O.

content.overview:
The SysOperation framework is D365 F&O's modern replacement for the legacy RunBase framework, providing a robust architecture for building batch operations, dialogs, and service endpoints. Unlike RunBase, SysOperation enforces clean separation of concerns through distinct classes: DataContract (parameters), Controller (orchestration), and Service (business logic). This framework powers critical D365 operations like posting routines, data imports, and integration services.

The framework's strength lies in its automatic serialization, client-server boundary handling, and seamless integration with the batch framework. It eliminates the boilerplate code required in RunBase while providing better testability, maintainability, and scalability. The framework automatically handles parameter validation, UI generation, and cross-tier communication, making it essential for enterprise-grade customizations.

SysOperation is particularly valuable when building operations that need to run in batch, require complex parameter sets, or serve as integration endpoints. Understanding its internal mechanics—especially the SysOperationServiceController lifecycle and data contract binding—is crucial for senior developers working on performance-critical or high-volume operations.
content.theory:
The SysOperation framework implements a Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) pattern with three core components. The SysOperationDataContractBase provides parameter encapsulation with automatic serialization using DataContractAttribute and DataMemberAttribute decorations. The SysOperationServiceController orchestrates execution flow, handling UI generation, parameter validation, and batch scheduling. The service class contains the business logic, decorated with SysEntryPointAttribute for security validation.

Internally, the framework leverages reflection extensively. The SysOperationServiceController.generateDialog() method uses SysOperationDataContractProcessorAttribute to build UI dialogs dynamically from data contract properties. The SysOperationDataContractProcessor class hierarchy (Base, Default, UIBuilder) transforms data contract metadata into DialogField objects. This reflection-based approach enables automatic UI generation but has performance implications that must be considered.

The framework integrates deeply with D365's batch infrastructure through BatchScheduler and SysOperationExecutionContext. When executed in batch mode, parameters are serialized using FormJsonSerializer, stored in BatchConstraintsHistory, and deserialized on the AOS during execution. The SysOperationProgress class provides standardized progress reporting, while SysOperationServiceController.run() manages the complete execution lifecycle including pre/post-processing hooks and exception handling.

code_examples.title: Complete SysOperation Implementation - Customer Credit Limit Update
code_examples.description: A production-ready SysOperation that demonstrates all framework components: data contract with validation, service with business logic, and proper error handling

code_examples.title: Advanced Controller with Custom Dialog and Batch Handling
code_examples.description: Custom controller implementation with sophisticated dialog customization, batch scheduling, and progress tracking

code_examples.title: Custom Data Contract Processor for Advanced UI Control
code_examples.description: Advanced data contract processor that demonstrates custom dialog field creation, dynamic lookups, and field dependencies

key_points: SysOperation enforces separation of concerns through DataContract (parameters), Controller (orchestration), and Service (business logic) — never mix these responsibilities
Use SysOperationDataContractProcessorAttribute to create custom UI processors for complex dialog requirements beyond basic field decoration
Implement proper batch handling with canGoBatch() returning true and use SysOperationProgress for user feedback in long-running operations
DataContract validation occurs at multiple levels: property setters, validate() method, and custom processor validateDataContract() — leverage all three appropriately
SysEntryPointAttribute(false) on service methods is critical for security — it ensures proper authorization checks are performed during execution
gotchas: Data contract properties MUST have DataMemberAttribute for serialization to work — missing attributes cause silent parameter loss in batch execution, leading to operations running with default values

Never perform database operations in data contract constructors or property setters — this causes performance issues during dialog generation and can fail in batch mode due to connection context differences
SysOperation controllers instantiated with new() instead of construct() bypass proper initialization — always use static construct() methods to ensure proper service binding and metadata loading
performance_tips: Minimize reflection-heavy operations in prePromptModifyContract() and data contract processors — cache lookup data and avoid repeated metadata queries that impact dialog responsiveness
Use ttsbegin/ttscommit batching in service methods for bulk operations — commit every 100-1000 records to balance transaction log growth with rollback granularity

quiz.q: What is the primary architectural advantage of SysOperation over RunBase framework?
options: Better performance through compiled queries
Enforced separation of concerns with distinct classes for parameters, control, and logic
Automatic transaction management
Built-in multi-threading support
quiz.explanation: SysOperation's key advantage is enforced separation of concerns through DataContract (parameters), Controller (orchestration), and Service (business logic) classes. This separation improves testability, maintainability, and enables better code reuse compared to RunBase's monolithic approach.

quiz.q: When does SysOperation framework serialize data contract parameters?
options: During batch execution and client-server boundary crossings
Only when storing to database
Every time a property is accessed
Only during dialog generation
quiz.explanation: SysOperation serializes data contract parameters during batch execution (to store in BatchConstraintsHistory) and when crossing client-server boundaries. This automatic serialization requires proper DataMemberAttribute decoration on all parameters that need persistence.

quiz.q: What happens if a data contract property lacks the DataMemberAttribute decoration?
options: Compilation error occurs
Runtime exception is thrown
Property value is lost during serialization/batch execution
Property becomes read-only
quiz.explanation: Properties without DataMemberAttribute are excluded from serialization, causing their values to be lost during batch execution or client-server communication. This is a silent failure that can cause operations to run with default/null values instead of user-specified parameters.

title: Dialogs: Dialog, DialogField, Lookups
summary: Master the Dialog framework for creating dynamic user input forms with custom fields, validations, and lookups in D365 F&O.

content.overview:
The Dialog framework in D365 F&O provides a powerful mechanism for creating runtime user input forms without requiring AOT form objects. Unlike static forms, Dialog objects can be constructed dynamically, making them ideal for batch jobs, reports, and utility functions that need user parameters. The framework consists of Dialog (container), DialogField (individual controls), and DialogRunbase (integration with RunBase pattern) classes that work together to create professional user interfaces.

Dialogs are particularly valuable when you need conditional field visibility, complex validation logic, or when integrating with the SysOperation framework. They're commonly used in report parameter dialogs, data import wizards, and maintenance utilities where the UI requirements are too dynamic for static forms but too complex for simple input dialogs.
content.theory:
The Dialog framework is built around the Dialog class (kernel class) which acts as a container for DialogField objects. Each DialogField represents a specific control type (string, integer, enum, lookup, etc.) and maintains its own state, validation, and events. The framework integrates deeply with the metadata system, automatically handling field properties like mandatory flags, extended data types, and help text.

Internally, Dialog uses the DialogForm class to render the actual form at runtime. The form is created dynamically using FormRun and FormBuildControl objects, which explains why dialogs have the same look and feel as AOT forms. The DialogField hierarchy includes specialized classes like DialogFieldString, DialogFieldInt, DialogFieldEnum, and DialogFieldLookup, each optimized for specific data types. The framework also supports advanced features like field groups, tabs, and custom validation through the validate() method pattern.

For enterprise scenarios, the SysOperationController and SysOperationServiceController classes provide Dialog integration with the SysOperation framework, enabling automatic UI generation from data contract classes and seamless integration with batch processing.

code_examples.title: Basic Dialog with Multiple Field Types
code_examples.description: Demonstrates creating a dialog with various field types including string, integer, enum, and date fields with proper validation

code_examples.title: Advanced Dialog with Custom Lookups and Field Dependencies
code_examples.description: Shows how to implement custom lookups, field dependencies, and dynamic field visibility based on user selections

key_points: Dialog objects are created at runtime and don't require AOT form objects — ideal for dynamic UIs and batch parameter collection
DialogField types include DialogFieldString, DialogFieldInt, DialogFieldEnum, DialogFieldLookup, each with type-specific methods and validation
Use registerOverrideMethod() to handle field events like modified() for implementing field dependencies and dynamic behavior
Dialog.lookupButton(2) enables standard lookup functionality, while performCustomLookup() allows completely custom lookup implementations
Always implement validate() method for business logic validation — it's called automatically when the user clicks OK
Dialog integrates seamlessly with RunBase pattern through DialogRunbase for batch job parameter collection and persistence
gotchas: Don't call dialog.run() multiple times — Dialog objects are single-use and will throw runtime errors on subsequent runs, create new instances instead
DialogField.value() returns the current field value but doesn't trigger validation — always call validate() explicitly before processing user input
Custom lookups require proper Query setup and SysTableLookup configuration — missing addLookupfield() calls result in empty lookup forms that confuse users
performance_tips: Cache DialogField references in class variables rather than calling dialog.field() repeatedly — field lookup by name is expensive in large dialogs
Use dialog.allowUpdateOnSelectCtrl(true) to enable real-time field updates, but be cautious with expensive validation logic in modified() methods

quiz.q: What is the correct way to make a DialogField mandatory and add lookup functionality?
options: dlgField.required(true); dlgField.addLookup();
dlgField.mandatory(true); dlgField.lookupButton(2);
dlgField.setMandatory(); dlgField.enableLookup();
dlgField.mustFill(true); dlgField.lookup(true);
quiz.explanation: DialogField.mandatory(true) sets the field as required, and lookupButton(2) enables the standard lookup button. The parameter 2 indicates a lookup button with dropdown arrow.

quiz.q: When implementing field dependencies in dialogs, where should you register field change event handlers?
options: In the validate() method
In the dialog() method immediately after creating fields
In the dialogPostRun() method after dialog initialization
In the constructor before creating the dialog
quiz.explanation: Event handlers should be registered in dialogPostRun() because the underlying form controls are not available until after the dialog is fully initialized and running.

quiz.q: What happens if you call dialog.run() on the same Dialog object twice?
options: The dialog displays again with the same values
The dialog displays with cleared/default values
A runtime error occurs because Dialog objects are single-use
The second call is ignored and returns false
quiz.explanation: Dialog objects are single-use in D365 F&O. Once run() completes, the internal form is disposed and cannot be reused. Always create a new Dialog instance for subsequent displays.

title: Table Maps & Map Extensions
summary: Master table maps for polymorphic data access patterns and learn how map extensions enable framework-agnostic code across multiple table implementations.

content.overview:
Table maps in D365 F&O provide a powerful polymorphic abstraction layer that allows X++ code to work uniformly across multiple tables sharing common fields and business logic. Unlike table inheritance, maps create a virtual interface that multiple concrete tables can implement, enabling framework-agnostic programming patterns essential for extensible applications.

Maps are particularly valuable in scenarios where you need to write generic business logic that operates on different tables with similar structures — such as dimension processing, workflow implementations, or integration patterns. They solve the fundamental challenge of code reusability when working with heterogeneous table structures that share common behavioral contracts.

Map extensions, introduced in later D365 versions, further enhance this capability by allowing partners and ISVs to extend existing maps with additional fields or methods without modifying the base application, maintaining upgradeability while enabling deep customizations.
content.theory:
Table maps are AOT objects that define a contract — a set of fields and methods — that concrete tables can implement by mapping their physical fields to the map's logical fields. The D365 kernel handles the runtime binding, translating map field references to actual table fields based on the current table context. This abstraction is implemented through the MapImplementationTable relationship in the AOT, where each participating table defines field mappings via the Mappings node.

The architecture leverages X++'s late binding capabilities. When you instantiate a map using a specific table (via the Map::table() method), the kernel creates a proxy that redirects field access and method calls to the underlying table implementation. This enables true polymorphism — your business logic remains unchanged whether operating on CustTable, VendTable, or any other table implementing the same map contract.

Map extensions utilize the standard D365 extensibility framework, allowing you to add fields through table extensions on the map itself, then extend the field mappings on participating tables. The kernel automatically handles the extended contract during runtime resolution, maintaining backward compatibility while enabling rich customizations.

code_examples.title: Basic Map Usage with AddressMap
code_examples.description: Demonstrates how to use the standard AddressMap to access address information polymorphically across Customer and Vendor tables

code_examples.title: Custom Map Implementation with Extensions
code_examples.description: Shows how to create a custom map for financial dimensions and extend it with additional fields

code_examples.title: Advanced Map Pattern with Runtime Table Discovery
code_examples.description: Demonstrates dynamic table discovery and map binding for extensible processing patterns

key_points: Maps provide polymorphic abstraction over multiple tables sharing common field contracts — use AddressMap, TaxMap, and other standard maps before creating custom ones
Field mappings are defined in AOT under each table's Mappings node — ensure proper mapping of logical map fields to physical table fields for correct runtime behavior
Map extensions allow adding fields to existing maps without modifying base code — extend the map table, then extend field mappings on implementing tables
Use Map::construct() and table() method for proper initialization — direct instantiation bypasses kernel's polymorphic binding mechanism
Runtime type checking with 'is' operator ensures safe casting when working with maps in generic processing scenarios
gotchas: Forgetting to call table() method after map construction results in null reference exceptions — the map must be bound to a concrete table instance before field access
Not all standard tables implement all maps — wrap map.table() calls in try/catch blocks to handle tables that don't implement the expected map contract
Map field mappings are cached at startup — changes to Mappings node require full IL generation and application restart to take effect in development
performance_tips: Cache map instances when processing multiple records from the same table — repeated Map::construct() calls add unnecessary overhead
Use specific map types rather than generic Common references to enable compile-time optimization and IntelliSense support

quiz.q: What is the correct way to initialize a map for use with a specific table?
options: map = new AddressMap(); map.init(table);
map = AddressMap::construct(); map.table(table);
map = AddressMap::find(table.RecId);
map = table.getMap(AddressMap::class);
quiz.explanation: Maps must be constructed using the construct() method, then bound to a table using table(). This enables the kernel's polymorphic field mapping.

quiz.q: What happens if you try to bind a map to a table that doesn't implement the map's contract?
options: The map returns default values for all fields
An Exception::Error is thrown
The operation silently fails with null values
The kernel automatically creates the mapping
quiz.explanation: When a table doesn't implement the required map contract (missing field mappings), the kernel throws Exception::Error during the table() method call.

quiz.q: How do map extensions maintain upgradeability while adding functionality?
options: They create new maps that inherit from base maps
They modify the original map metadata directly
They extend the map table and add field mappings on implementing tables
They use reflection to dynamically add fields at runtime
quiz.explanation: Map extensions work by extending the map table itself (adding fields), then extending the Mappings node on each implementing table to map the new fields, maintaining the original map contract while adding functionality.

title: Global Class & Utility Methods
summary: Learn how to create and use the Global class for housing static utility methods that provide system-wide functionality in D365 F&O.

content.overview:
The Global class in D365 F&O serves as a centralized repository for static utility methods that can be accessed from anywhere in the system without instantiation. This class provides essential helper functions for common operations like string manipulation, date calculations, number formatting, and system utilities. Understanding the Global class is fundamental for D365 development as it promotes code reuse, maintains consistency across the application, and provides a standardized way to implement common business logic.

The Global class follows a static-only pattern where all methods are declared as static, making them accessible via Global::methodName() syntax. This approach is particularly valuable for utility functions that don't require object state and can operate purely on their input parameters. Microsoft has extensively used this pattern throughout the standard application, and custom implementations should follow the same architectural principles.
content.theory:
In the D365 F&O AOT structure, the Global class (located under Classes node) is a special system class that's automatically available throughout the application without import statements or explicit references. The class inherits from Object but should only contain static methods to maintain the utility pattern. The Global class is loaded early in the application startup sequence, ensuring its methods are available for framework initialization and runtime operations.

The architectural significance lies in the Global class being part of the kernel layer, making it accessible from both client and server tiers. When developing custom Global methods, consider that they execute in the tier where they're called unless explicitly decorated with server/client attributes. The class supports method overloading but not method overriding since all methods should be static. Microsoft's standard Global class contains over 200 utility methods covering areas like string operations (Global::strFmt), date manipulation (Global::utcDateTime2SystemDateTime), mathematical operations (Global::round), and system functions (Global::info, Global::error).

code_examples.title: Basic Global Class Utility Methods
code_examples.description: Creating custom utility methods in the Global class for common business operations like tax calculation and string formatting

code_examples.title: Advanced Global Methods with Error Handling
code_examples.description: Production-grade Global methods demonstrating proper error handling, logging, and integration with D365 framework classes

key_points: Global class methods must always be declared as static and should not maintain any instance state between calls
Use proper parameter validation and null checks at the beginning of each Global method to prevent runtime errors
Implement comprehensive error handling with try/catch blocks and provide meaningful error messages using Global::error() or Global::warning()
Follow naming conventions with descriptive method names and use XML documentation for IntelliSense support in the development environment
Consider thread safety when implementing caching mechanisms or static variables in Global methods, especially for server-side operations
gotchas: Never create instance variables in the Global class — this breaks the utility pattern and can cause memory leaks since the Global class is never garbage collected
Avoid calling non-static methods from Global methods without proper instantiation — this will cause compilation errors and violates the static-only principle
Don't perform database operations in Global methods without proper transaction handling — use ttsbegin/ttscommit when modifying data to maintain ACID properties
performance_tips: Cache expensive calculations or database lookups in static variables with appropriate cache invalidation logic to avoid repeated operations
Use StringBuilder for string concatenation operations instead of repeated string additions to prevent memory fragmentation

quiz.q: What is the correct way to declare a method in the Global class?
options: public void calculateTax(Amount _amount)
public static real calculateTax(Amount _amount)
private static void calculateTax(Amount _amount)
protected real calculateTax(Amount _amount)
quiz.explanation: Why B is correct: Global class methods must be declared as public static to be accessible from anywhere in the application. The static keyword ensures the method can be called without instantiating the class, and public visibility allows system-wide access.

quiz.q: Which of the following is a best practice when implementing error handling in Global methods?
options: Use throw statements without try/catch blocks
Return null values without logging errors
Use try/catch blocks with Global::error() for logging
Ignore exceptions to prevent application crashes
quiz.explanation: Why C is correct: Proper error handling in Global methods requires try/catch blocks to gracefully handle exceptions and Global::error() to log meaningful error messages. This approach provides better debugging capabilities and maintains application stability.

quiz.q: What happens if you try to create instance variables in the Global class?
options: The variables work normally like in any other class
The compiler generates a warning but allows compilation
It breaks the utility pattern and can cause memory issues
The variables are automatically converted to static
quiz.explanation: Why C is correct: Creating instance variables in the Global class violates the static utility pattern and can cause memory leaks since the Global class instance is never garbage collected. All Global class members should be static methods only.

title: Label Files & Multi-Language Support
summary: Learn how to implement proper multi-language support in D365 F&O using label files, best practices for label creation, and runtime label resolution mechanisms.

content.overview: Label files are the cornerstone of multi-language support in Dynamics 365 Finance & Operations, enabling applications to display text in the user's preferred language without code changes. Unlike hardcoded strings, labels are externalized text resources stored in .ald files within the AOT, automatically managed by the kernel for runtime language switching. Understanding label architecture is crucial for enterprise D365 implementations serving global organizations, as improper label usage can lead to maintenance nightmares, translation issues, and poor user experience across different locales.
content.theory: D365 F&O's label system is built on the SysLabel framework, where each label has a unique identifier (like @SYS12345) mapped to translations in various languages. Labels are organized into label files (e.g., Global.ald, Foundation.ald) containing thousands of label IDs with their corresponding text values for each supported language. The kernel automatically resolves labels at runtime using the current user's language preference from UserInfo.language, falling back to the system's default language when translations are missing. Label files are compiled into the AOT and distributed with model packages, making them part of the application's metadata. The SysLabel class provides programmatic access to label resolution, while design-time tools in Visual Studio enable label creation, editing, and validation. Each label file has a three-letter prefix (like SYS, HRM, FIN) that becomes part of the label ID, ensuring uniqueness across modules and reducing collision risks in large development teams.

code_examples.title: Creating and Using Labels in Forms
code_examples.description: Demonstrates proper label creation and usage in form controls with fallback handling

code_examples.title: Dynamic Label Resolution and Language Switching
code_examples.description: Advanced label manipulation including runtime language changes and programmatic label creation

key_points: Label IDs follow the format @FilePrefix:LabelNumber (e.g., @SYS318892) and must be unique within the label file
Use SysLabel::labelId2String() for runtime label resolution, which automatically respects the user's language preference
Always create labels through Visual Studio's label editor to ensure proper AOT integration and avoid duplicate IDs
Label files are compiled into models and distributed as metadata, not as separate resource files like .NET applications
Missing translations fall back to the system default language (typically en-us), never leaving users with untranslated label IDs
gotchas: Never hardcode user-facing text strings — always use labels, as hardcoded strings cannot be translated and violate D365 best practices
Don't concatenate labels at runtime for complex messages — create complete sentence labels instead to maintain proper grammar across languages
Avoid checking if SysLabel::labelId2String() returns the original label ID to detect missing labels, as this doesn't work reliably in all scenarios — use proper error handling instead
performance_tips: Label resolution is cached by the kernel, so repeated calls to the same label ID don't incur database lookups
Avoid creating labels dynamically in loops — pre-resolve labels before iteration and reuse the resolved strings

quiz.q: What is the correct format for a label ID in D365 F&O?
quiz.explanation: Why @SYS:318892 is correct: Label IDs follow the format @FilePrefix:LabelNumber, where the colon separates the three-letter file prefix from the numeric identifier.

quiz.q: Which method should you use to resolve a label ID to its text value at runtime?
options: SysLabel::labelId2String()
Global::label2Text()
SysLabelFile::resolve()
Label::getText()
quiz.explanation: Why SysLabel::labelId2String() is correct: This is the standard kernel method that handles language fallback, caching, and proper label resolution for the current user's language preference.

quiz.q: What happens when a label translation is missing for the user's preferred language?
options: An exception is thrown
The system falls back to the default language
An empty string is returned
The label ID itself is displayed
quiz.explanation: Why fallback to default language is correct: D365 F&O automatically falls back to the system default language (typically en-us) when a translation is missing, ensuring users never see raw label IDs.
